coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots embedded metal organic framework @ chitosan core-shell nanoparticles for vitro dual mode imaging and pH-responsive drug delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Caixue
Sun, Keke
Zhang, Cheng
Tan, Tingyuan
Xu, Mingyue
Liu, Yana
Xu, Chen
Wang, Yingxi
Li, Ling
Whittaker, Andrew
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel core-shell MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanoparticles were synthesized and validated for in vitro diagnosis and treatment of cancer. MIL-100 (Fe-BTC) is a potential drug platform because of its large specific surface area. Importantly it can be used for magnetic resonance imaging as it contains large amount of Fe3+. To demonstrate the optical imaging properties, carbon dots (CDs) were encapsulated into the synthesized MOFs, thereby endowing fluorescence features to the nanoparticles. The composite was further coated with O-carboxymethyl chitosan (OCMC) to form MOFs/CDs@OCMC to improve biocompability and endow pH responsive property. The structure and morphology of the composite were characterized by XRD, IR, SEM and TEM. Moreover, the composite exhibited excellent performance in fluorescence imaging (FOI) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), which proved that MOFs/CDs@OCMC could be used for FOI/MRI dual mode imaging. Cytotoxicity assays indicated that MOFs/CDs@OCMC particles were highly biocompatible and suitable to be used to transport drugs in human bodies. Doxorubicin (DOX) was selected as a model drug and the drug delivery was discussed. The drug loading was 50 mg/g. It was found that the DOX was released rapidly at pH 3.8 but at pH 7.4, the rate and extent of release were greatly attenuated. The particles therefore demonstrated an excellent pH-triggered drug release performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: O-carboxymethyl chitosan
Carbon dots
MIL-100
Dual model imaging
pH responsive release

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) MOFs; (b) MOFs/CDs; (c) and (d) MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanocomposites.
TEM images of (a) MOFs; (b) MOFs/CDs; (c) (d) MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanocomposites.
The zeta potential of MOFs, MOFs/CDs and MOFs/CDs@OCMC
Cell viability of MOFs, MOFs/CDs, MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanocomposites.
Photoluminescence spectra of the (a) CDs and (b) MOFs/CDs@OCMC
Fluorescence imaging and magnetic resonance imaging of (a)MOFs/CDs and (b) MOFs/CDs@OCMC.
Drug loading capacities of MOFs, MOFs/CDs and MOFs/CDs@OCMC
UV–vis absorption of DOX solution before and after interaction with MOFs/CDs@OCMC
pH responsive release of DOX@ (a) MOFs/CDs and (b) MOFs/CDs@OCMC
Schematic illustrated the preparation of the MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanocomposites and its application as drug carrier of DOX for treatment of cancer.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots embedded metal organic framework @ chitosan core-shell nanoparticles for vitro dual mode imaging and pH-responsive drug delivery

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel core-shell MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanoparticles were synthesized and validated for in vitro diagnosis and treatment of cancer. MIL-100 (Fe-BTC) is a potential drug platform because of its large specific surface area. Importantly it can be used for magnetic resonance imaging as it contains large amount of Fe3+. To demonstrate the optical imaging properties, carbon dots (CDs) were encapsulated into the synthesized MOFs, thereby endowing fluorescence features to the nanoparticles. The composite was further coated with O-carboxymethyl chitosan (OCMC) to form MOFs/CDs@OCMC to improve biocompability and endow pH responsive property. The structure and morphology of the composite were characterized by XRD, IR, SEM and TEM. Moreover, the composite exhibited excellent performance in fluorescence imaging (FOI) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), which proved that MOFs/CDs@OCMC could be used for FOI/MRI dual mode imaging. Cytotoxicity assays indicated that MOFs/CDs@OCMC particles were highly biocompatible and suitable to be used to transport drugs in human bodies. Doxorubicin (DOX) was selected as a model drug and the drug delivery was discussed. The drug loading was 50 mg/g. It was found that the DOX was released rapidly at pH 3.8 but at pH 7.4, the rate and extent of release were greatly attenuated. The particles therefore demonstrated an excellent pH-triggered drug release performance.

ce:section.ce:para: A main challenge for cancer therapy is the engineering of a smart nanocarrier that can competently encapsulate drug molecules at high loading meanwhile control their release at target sites [1–3]. With the development of modern medicine, drug carriers are strict with their properties and functions. Except for high loading of drug molecules during the treatment, the early detection of cancer by using smart drug carriers is also in highly demand. At present, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is a noninvasive diagnostic technique with excellent spatial resolution and has become one of the most important clinical tools for visualizing the internal structures of the body in detail [4]. Therefore, many researchers are devoted to exploiting new types of drug carriers which can be applied as MRI contrast agents for early detection of disease. Among the studies of MRI contrast agents, much effort has been focused on the development of MOFs-based imaging agents because these materials can easily carry large amounts of paramagnetic metal ions such as Fe3+ or Gd3+ which is readily provide enhanced contrast of the disease area.

As a new porous material, Metal Organic Frameworks (MOFs) has aroused the interest of the majority of researchers owing to their potential application in many areas, especially for bioactive molecule delivery. MOFs have drawn considerable attention as a platform for drug delivery due to their ultrahigh porosity, large surface areas and tunable functionality [5–8]. Compared to widely reported porous materials (e.g. silica), the synthetic procedure of MOFs is much simpler and more efficient [9,10]. Another advantage is that the pore structure of MOFs can be controlled by changing the conditions of reaction or the selection of different metal ions and ligands. Moreover, the functions of MOFs can be altered by simply changing the ions to construct the framework. For example, MOFs that constructed with paramagnetic metal ions, such as Fe3+, Gd3+ and Mn2+ can be used as 1H MRI contrast agent. MIL-100 (MIL stands for Material of Institute Lavoisier) is an ideal MRI contrast agent since the framework of MOFs carry large amounts of paramagnetic metal ions of Fe3+. Within the nano MOFs studied in the referred studies, the mesoporous iron (III) trimesate, called MIL-100 appears to date as the most promising one, in terms of green synthesis, physicochemical stability, in vitro and in vivo toxicity, high loading capacities and good degradation/release profile. However, although MRI possesses high resolution and excellent soft tissue contrast, the relative low sensitivity is a limitation [11]. Therefore, another imaging modality with high sensitivity, such as fluorescence optical imaging, can be further incorporated to MOFs.

Fluorescence optical imaging (FOI) is a useful diagnostic method and there are several choices to make materials as FOI contrast agents, such as rare earth element [12], organic fluorescent dyes and inorganic fluorescent dyes [13,14]. However, most of these fluorescent materials are not suitable for cell and tissue imaging due to their potential side effect to human bodies. Over the past decades, quantum dots have been tested in many biotechnological applications for their excellent fluorescence, such as DNA array technology, immunofluorescence assays as well as cell and animal biology. In fact, it is also widely reported that many quantum dots are not suitable for in vivo studies because of their potential toxicity to organism. Carbon quantum dots (CDs), a novel class of nano carbons, have been attracting increasing attention due to their unique properties, such as excellent up-converting,size dependent photoluminescence, robust chemical inertness, superior stability against photo bleaching, high aqueous solubility and high compatibility [15–20]. CDs have been successfully utilized in bioimaging, sensors and light-emitting devices [21–23]. As FOI contrast agents, CDs have the advantages of high sensitivity and a relatively low cost but suffer from the limited penetration depth in tissue and lack of anatomic resolution. In order to obtain accurate diagnosis information, FOI/MRI dual mode imaging would be an effective method since the combination of FOI and MRI displays excellent soft tissue contrast, high spatial resolution and sensitivity thus compensating for the disadvantages of both techniques.

DOX is an anthracycline antibiotic which has been widely used for cancer chemotherapy. However, its clinical application has been limited by its dose-related side effects such as bone marrow toxicity, gastrointestinal disorders, acute or cumulative cardiotoxicity, and extravasation. In order to minimize the side effects and improve the therapeutic efficiency of DOX, the controlled drug release techniques are in demand. Chitosan (CS) is a biocompatible and biodegradable natural polysaccharide derived from chitin and it is increasingly used for various biomedical applications such as drug and gene delivery, tissue engineering, wound healing, and antimicrobial property [24–28]. Lately, chitosan has been reported to be able to enhance degradability and drug loading and improve pH-responsive drug release efficiency [29,30]. Furthermore, through the modification of chitosan can effectively improve their biocompatibility and provide further support for their applications in the area of biomedicine.

In present study, we reported a newly designed MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanocomposites loaded with DOX for anticancer drug delivery and FOI/MRI dual-mode imaging. Scheme 1 illustrates the structure of MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanocomposites and its application as “smart” drug carrier for DOX. The preparation of this system is composed with three steps. Firstly, MIL-100 was synthesized by hydrothermal method. Second, then CDs were encapsulated in the pore of MIL-100 by hydrothermal method. Finally, the composites were coated with O-carboxymethyl chitosan (OCMC). Here, The OCMC is applied as pH-responsive segments for controllable release of DOX under acidic environments in tumor and the carbon quantum dots are used for cell imaging due to their photo stability and sharper spectra. The porous structure of the MIL-100 in the core allows high efficiency of loading drug molecules and the Fe3+ can make the composite used as a T2-weighted MRI contrast agent. In summary, for the first time, biocompatible carbon dots with high fluorescence are conjugated on the metal organic framework and coated with chitosan for drug delivery and FOI/MRI dual-mode imaging. The potential utility of such a nano delivery system (MOFs/CDs@OCMC) in FOI/MRI dual mode imaging and anticancer therapy are demonstrated strongly by in vitro experiments.

Trimesic acid (H3BTC), FeCl3·6H2O, dextran, O-carboxymethyl chitosan (OCMC) and Doxorubicin (DOX) were all purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Reagents and solvents were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Fresh double distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

The power X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed using a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker Company, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was carried out on a Single frequency infrared spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, USA). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained through a JSM6510LV scanning electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). And transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were obtained using a H7500 transmission electron microscope (Hitachi, Japan).

0.14 g H3BTC (0.67 mmol) and 0.27 g FeCl3·6H2O (1 mmol) were added to 30 mL water. After stirring at room temperature for 30 min, the mixture was then heated to 160 °C for 24 h in a Teflon reaction vessel. The formed precipitate was collected by filtration, and then washed with water and ethanol for three times. The product, MIL-100, was obtained and dried at 100 °C for another 2 h. The textural structure of MIL-100 can be proved by spherical aberration-corrected HRTEM, a shown in Fig. S1. It is clear that MIL-100 has the form of uniformly shaped spherical particles with an average particle size of about 200 nm and the lattice space of MIL-100 is 3.126 nm. On basis of MIL-100, the composite was prepared.

0.12 g dextran was added in 30 mL water and stirred until completely dissolved. Then 0.05 g synthesized MIL-100 was dispersed completely in the dextran solution and the mixture was then heated to 160 °C for 24 h in a Teflon reaction vessel. The product, MOFs/CDs, was collected and washed in the same way. After that, 0.1 g OCMC and 0.05 g MOFs/CDs were dispersed in 50 mL water separately. Then MOFs/CDs solution was slowly added to OCMC solution with stirring rapidly and constant 40 °C. Keep stirring and constant temperature for 12 h after dripping completely. The product, MOFs/CDs@OCMC, was washed and collected.

0.025 g MOFs/CDs@OCMC were added into a culture bottle with 20 mL doxorubicin water solution (0.1 g/L). After being placed at room temperature for 24 h, 48 h and 72 h, a UV–vis spectrophotometer was used to determine the concentration of residual DOX at the calibrated maximum wavelength of 480 nm.

The drug release experiment of DOX was performed in a glass apparatus at 37.0 °C in pH 3.8 and pH 7.4 buffer solution respectively. MOFs/CDs@OCMC (0.01 g) containing a known amount of DOX were suspended in buffer solution. The mixture solution was mechanically stirred and the temperature was maintained at 37.0 °C in a water bath. The drug release medium (3 mL) was withdrawn for analysis by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy at a wavelength of 480 nm at given time intervals and replaced with the same volume of fresh buffer solution.

The biocompatibility of the MOFs, MOFs/CDs and MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanoparticles were assessed by the 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-(3-carboxymethoxyphenyl)-2-(4-sulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium, inner salt (MTS) assay. The MCF-7 cells were grown in DMEM culture medium containing 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotic-antimycotic (AA). The MCF-7 cells were incubated at 37.0 °C with 5% CO2/95% air. The cells were seeded with these nanoparticles (concentrations from 0 μg/mL to 200 μg/mL) on a 96-well plate in a final volume of 200 μL with 20000 cells per well for 24 h. The MTS solution (20 μL) was added into each well, and the cells were further incubated for additional 4 h. After shaking the microplate briefly on a shaker, the absorbance of treated and untreated cells at 490 nm was measured via a microplate reader. Untreated cells in medium were used as control group.

To determine the MRI transverse relaxivity, MOFs/CDs and MOFs/CDs@OCMC were diluted in distilled water with iron concentrations in the range of 0–2.5 mmol/L. The samples were transferred to a series of 400 μL sample tubes and placed in a preclinical MRI system (Bruker, Germany), comprising a 300 mm bore 7 T ClinScan, running Siemens VB17. A 86 mm rf coil was used to acquire the images. T2 estimation was performed using a multiple contrast spin echo sequence with the following parameters: number of slices = 1, slice thickness = 2 mm, field-of-view = 50 × 50 mm, acquisition matrix = 192 × 192, TR = 5000 ms, TE times = 30–600 ms in 30 ms increments. Relaxation rates (1/T2) were d plotted against the concentrations of Fe. The r2 relaxivities were then obtained from the slope of these curves.

The in vitro optical imaging experiments were performed on an in vivo MS FX Pro instrument (now supplied by Bruker Corporation). Images were collected with a 400 nm excitation and 550 nm emission filter set (f-stop 2.80, 2 × 2 binning, 120 mm FOV, 10 s exposure time). All images were batch exported as 16-bit TIFF images and image processing was completed using Image-J (National Institutes of Health). Fluorescence images were false colored.

The crystallinity of the prepared materials were initially examined by the XRD method, as shown in Fig. S2. It was noted that the XRD characteristic diffraction peaks of MIL-100 well matched the calculated patterns of MIL-100, proving the successful synthesis of MIL-100. In the case of MOFs/CDs, a slight broad band at around 2θ = 25°–28° can be assigned to the amorphous CDs doping into the MOFs structure. The sharp characteristic diffraction peaks of MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanocomposites were observed at the same position as compared to that of the MIL-100, but all the peak height decreased remarkably since the surface of the MOFs was coated with OCMC. Besides, other peaks were remained as the same with the MOFs, which confirmed that neither CDs doping nor OCMC coating changed any crystalline phase of MIL-100.

The molecular structure of materials was further confirmed by the infrared spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S3. The bending vibration peaks of –CH on the benzene ring could be observed at 759 cm−1, 712 cm−1 and 575 cm−1 in the infrared spectrum. The characteristic peaks at 1571 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1 could be assigned to the asymmetric stretching peaks and symmetric stretching vibration peaks of carboxyl group in H3BTC. The characteristic absorption peak of H3BTC had an obvious blue shift (1720 cm−1), which indicated that there was coordination between Fe3+ and the carboxyl group of H3BTC. The characteristic absorption peaks of the MOFs/CDs were basically the same in the infrared spectrum as compared to that of MOFs, which could be all attributed to the characteristic peaks of MOFs. The peak located at 3500 cm−1 was attributed to the hydroxyl groups of MIL-100. There was a broad peak at 3400 cm−1 both in MOFs/CDs and MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanocomposites, which can be explained by the association of hydroxyl groups. There were a large number of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in CDs, which further proved the presence of CDs on the composites.

Except for the characteristic absorption peaks mentioned above, the bending vibration mode of N–H and the C–N stretching vibration mode at 1613 cm−1 and 1243 cm−1 could be observed in the MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanocomposites, which was attributed to the primary amine groups of OCMC. This fact indicated that OCMC was coated on the surface of MOFs/CDs successfully.

The morphology, surface texture of the prepared materials were then measured by scanning electron microscope (SEM) images, as shown in Fig. 1 . MIL-100 exhibited sphere structure and the particle size was about 200 nm, which is in accordance with the result of HRTEM of MIL-100. After formation of the MOFs/CDS, spheres accumulated to form the cluster structure (about 600 nm). This may be due to the hydrophilic carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the CDs, which are more likely to generate hydrogen bonds with the carboxyl groups of the MOFs, thereby making the particles easier to aggregate. Since the size of CDs is very tiny and is hard to be observed by SEM. The surface of MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanoparticles changed smoother and only a few pores could be observed after being coated by OCMC.

The TEM images of the materials were shown in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2 a showed sphere structure of MIL-100. From Fig. 2 b, particles with diameter at about 5 nm could observed in the TEM image of MOFs. The image of MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanoparticles showed obvious core-shell structure and the OCMC shell was around 2–3 nm (Fig. 2 c and d). The CDs were also observed in the structure of the composites. Consider the feature of easy aggregation of MIL-100 and MIL-100/CDs particles, the MOFs/CDs@OCMC composites was well distributed in ethanol since the OCMC improved the dispersion of the material. All these experimental results proved that the MOFs/CDs@OCMC composites with the expected structure were synthesized successfully.

The success synthesis can be further proved by the zeta potential, as shown in Fig. 3 . The zeta potential of MOFs (MIL-101) was −21mV, because the carboxyl group of the trimesic acid in MOFs is with negative charge. The surface of CDs is always with carboxyl group and hydroxyl group, and the negative charged groups resulted in the more negative Zeta potential −30 mV after the formation of the hybrid MOFs/CDs. It can be seen that the potential of MOFs/CDs@OCMC changed to −13 mV, which can be explained that the positive charged group imino of OCMC increased the zeta potential. Moreover, the zeta potential increased from −13mV to 2 mV when then condition became acidic. The protonation of imino and hydroxyl groups of OCMC will make the MOFs/CDs@OCMC with more positive charges. It also indicates that pH value has effect the on the charge of MOFs/CDs@OCMC, which will be used for pH responsive drug release because the protonation will change the interaction between drugs and MOFs/CDs@OCMC.

The cytotoxicity of drug delivery system was one of the important evaluation factors for its biomaterial application. Herein, MTS assays were carried on MCF-7 cell lines. Fig. 4 represented the results of MCF-7 cells incubated with MOFs, MOFs/CDs and MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanocomposites at different concentrations (0, 25, 50, 100, 150, 200 μg/mL). After incubation with these particles for 24 h, the cellular viability were all greater than 80%, suggesting that MOFs, MOFs/CDs and MOFs/CDs@OCMC were of low cytotoxicity, which enabled them to be used as the delivery vehicle for drugs. In particular, the MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanocomposites were found to be nontoxic even at a high sample concentration at 200 μg/mL with a cell viability higher than 95%, which is much higher than that of the MOFs and MOFs/CDs samples. The result indicated that the OCMC coating was able to enhance the biocompatibility, which enable the composite more suitable for drug delivery.

In order to understand the fluorescence behavior of the CDs and MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanocomposites, photoluminescence study was performed under 360 nm excitation, which is shown in Fig. 5 . The results showed that both the CDs and MOFs/CDs@OCMC samples exhibited strong emission behavior. There is a strong emission peak at 450 nm in the photoluminescence spectra of CDs as shown in Fig. 5 a. Bright blue luminescence was observed with UV (365 nm) light excitation in aqueous solution, as displayed in the inset of Fig. 5 a. The emission peak of the MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanocomposites shifted to 485 nm as compared to that of the CDs and it may be due to the conjugated effect of H3BTC in MIL-100.

To demonstrate the multimodal imaging performances of the MOFs/CDs and MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanocomposites, the solution fluorescence imaging and magnetic resonance imaging were performed, as shown in Fig. 6 . Obviously green fluorescence optical images of the MOFs/CDs and MOFs/CDs@OCMC in aqueous solution can be obtained with 410 nm excitation. The fluorescence images of the MOFs/CDs and MOFs/CDs@OCMC showed dose-dependent contrast enhancements, demonstrating the potential application as a FOI mode contrast agent.

Similarly, the changes in MRI signals could be observed by the reinforcement of the spot darkness upon gradually increase of the concentration of MOFs/CDs. This is caused by the nature of the MOFs/CDs as negative MRI contrast agents which can shorten the transverse (T2) relaxation time of water, resulting in a hypointense signal on T2-weighted MRI. It was well known that relaxivity is normalized relaxation rates of a water molecule, as shown in equation 1/T = r [Fe] +1/T0. Where, T0 was the relaxation time of the medium in the absence of contrast agent, [Fe] was the concentration of iron, and T was the relaxation time of the medium with contrast agent. To quantitatively compare the efficiencies of the MOFs/CDs as MRI contrast agents, the transverse relaxivity (r2) was measured at a field strength of 9.4 T and it exhibited r2 at 74.61 mM−1 s−1. Furthermore, MOFs/CDs@OCMC also exhibits green fluorescence optical imaging and the value of transverse relaxivity (r2) was 19.83 mM−1 s−1, which indicated that the MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanocomposites can be used for a MRI mode contrast agent. Samples with higher concentrations of nanoparticles in solution show brighter fluorescence and darker MRI images, which clearly demonstrate the potential application as a FOI/MRI dual mode contrast agent [31].

To demonstrate the potential medical application of the prepared composites, the performance of loading and release of DOX were then examined. DOX loading capacities results were shown in Fig. 7 . We noticed that the drug loading of MOFs/CDs@OCMC (50 mg/g) was much larger than that of MOFs (15 mg/g). The hydrogen bond can be formed between the large amount of –NH2 and –OH of OCMC and the –NH2 of DOX. The strong interaction largely increased the drug loading capacity of the composite.

The successful DOX loading was investigated by UV–vis and the results were illustrated in Fig. 8 . Obviously decrease in absorption spectra of DOX solution was found and the color of DOX became light after drug loading. That indicated the DOX loaded the composite successfully. As the result, the MOFs/CDs@OCMC composite was explored as the potential carrier to transport DOX.

Next, we examined the controlled release of DOX@MOFs/CDs and DOX@MOFs/CDs @OCMC in PBS buffer solution at different pH levels, and the results were shown in Fig. 9 . About 20% DOX could be released from MIL-100/CDs slowly after 60 h both in pH 3.8 and pH 7.4. As for MOFs/CDs @OCMC, 20% release of DOX@MOFs/CDs@OCMC after 50 h in pH 7.4, completely DOX release in 60 h was observed in pH 3.8 buffer solution. That indicated OCMC coated on the materials could endow the composite pH responsive.

The pH value has effect the on the charge of MOFs/CDs@OCMC, which was proved by Zeta potential, as shown in Fig. 3. The protonation of MOFs/CDs@OCMC in acid environment will change the interaction between drugs and MOFs/CDs@OCMC. The mechanism of pH responsible release is caused by the change of interaction between DOX and MOFs/CDs@OCMC in different pH conditions, as shown in Fig. 10 .

The large number of –OH and –NH2 on the OCMC and DOX formed strong hydrogen bonding interaction in drug loading experiment and the hydrogen bonding was hardly destroyed at pH 7.4 solution. When the release experiment was performed at pH 3.8 solution, the acid medium destroyed the hydrogen bonding between OCMC and DOX. Besides, the large number of –OH and –NH2 on the OCMC were with positive electricity because of the protonation of imino and hydroxyl groups at pH 3.8 solution, and the OCMC molecular were expanding since like charges repel each other. Therefore, DOX could hardly release from drug carrier at pH 7.4 while easily release at pH 3.8. Considering the acidic of tumor cells displayed, MOFs/CDs@OCMC could be employed as the transport vehicle for effective tumor chemotherapy as the result of high loading capacity and pH triggered release.

In this work, MOFs/CDs@OCMC particles were designed and successfully synthesized with a view to efficient loading and controlled release of DOX. The experimental results confirmed formation of MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanoparticles with a core-shell structure, high drug loading, and excellent pH-responsive release behavior. The negligible cytotoxicity of MOFs/CDs@OCMC particles towards human normal cells enable them to be used as the potential drug carrier and imaging contrast. Furthermore, both FOI and MRI results indicated the potential FOI/MRI dual mode imaging of the MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanoparticles. It can be concluded that MOFs/CDs@OCMC nanoparticles have great potential for FOI/MRI dual-mode imaging and pH-responsive drug delivery of anticancer drugs. It was expected that the success of the present findings will provide an ideal material for the integration of diagnosis and treatment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (No. 2017CFB530) and Wuhan Morning Light Plan of Youth Science and Technology (2017050304010282). The authors acknowledge the Australian Research Council (CE140100036, DP0987407, DP110104299, LE0775684, LE0668517, and LE0882357) and the National Health and Medical Research Council (APP1021759) for funding of this research. The Australian National Fabrication Facility, Queensland Node, is also acknowledged for access to some items of equipment. The National Imaging Facility is acknowledged for the access of ClinScan. C.Z. acknowledges the University of Queensland for his Early Career Researcher Grant (UQECR1720289).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In-vitro release study of hydrophobic drug using electrospun cross-linked gelatin nanofibers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Laha, S. Yadav, S. Majumdar, C.S. Sharma, In-vitro release study of hydrophobic drug using electrospun cross-linked gelatin nanofibers, Biochem. Eng. J. 105 (2016) 481-488.
A. Dass, R.Guo , J.B. Tracy, R. Balasubramanian, A.D. Douglas, R.W. Murraym, Gold nanoparticles with perfluorothiolate ligands, Langmuir 24 (2007) 310-315.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Combinational delivery of c-myc siRNA and nucleoside analogs in a single, synthetic nanocarrier for targeted cancer therapy

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ion-responsive 19F MRI contrast agents for the detection of cancer cell
